object, but having been appointed Guardian of Louvain College and
Professor of Theology, he had not had the leisure requisite to arrange

of the Four. Masters, was assigned by his superiors the duty of going

Order at Louvain, had been a genealogist by profession, of great re-

‘““ Acta Sanctorum’ that DBrother Michael ‘“ after he had embraced
our Seraphic Order in this Convent of Louvain he was employed as
Coadjutor, and was sent back by his superiors to Ireland to search out
and obtain the lives of the saints and other sacred antiquities of his
country, which are for the most part written in the language of his
country and very ancient. He copied many lives of the saints, genealo-
gies, three or four different and ancient martyrologies, and many other
monuments of great antiquity, which copied anew be transmitted to
Louvain.”” While Rrother Michael was engaged in this work he cou-
trived also to amass and copy works relating to the past history, of
Ireland, many of which he subsequently used in the compilation of the
Annals of the Four Masters. The MSS., etc., used in connection with
the ‘‘ Annals,” as well as the ‘“ Acta Sanctorum,” were deposited at

~ Loumin, but Brother Michael died some few months before the ‘¢ Acta

YEATS, SYNGE AND “THE PLAYBOY.”

The remark was once made of Oscar Wilde that he might have been
Parnell’s successor. Of another Irishman, who 18 only known to the
~ mass as a man of letters, Mr. W. B. Yeats, the biographer will prob-
: ~ably say that an Irish leader, a Grattan perhaps rather than a Parnell
- —an orator—was lost in a poet. That will not be strictly true, for,
in fact, Mr. Yeats has done much for Ireland, aside, I mean, from his
- actual literary achievement. . . . It is not possible for an Irish

writer working in Ireland to live detachedly. Synge returning home
~after a long absence did not realise this; the row over ‘‘ The Playboy
_ bewildered him utterly ; when he was asked) what he thought of it he
- could only say, ‘‘It's an extravaganza. I don’t care a rap. 2 Mrs
Yeats, on the other hand, immediately found confirmation of his phil-

Oﬂoiphy of Irish history, and was prepared to agree with Synge’s enemies
" *‘tha.t ‘“The Playboy,” or rather its reception in Dublin, truly raised a
fnatmnal issue. . . . I ask myself whom we have to thank for
Synge and ‘“ The Playboy of the Western World.”” It happens that I
_ can recall the first week of the production of *‘ The Playboy” in Dub-

PEN PORTRAITS.

AL 1s Georgo Russell ; and George Russell, taking him all round,
18 the finest mind and one of the most vital personahities in the Irelan
of to-day. In his poems he is a weaver of beauty and mysticism ; as 3
painter he has vision and tenderness, spirituality and daring; he writes
prose that I will match against any man’s for ease and force and clear-
ness; his talk is a fertilising stream of wit and whimsy and pungent
Irish commousense ; he is one of the two living Irishmen who dare to
mix their polities with humour; on land banks and creameries and the
problems of rural life he is an unequalled authority; and his main
vocation is that of editing an agricultural paper.

Shaggy and big and hearded, with a whiff of Montmartre about him,
1t is not often that he descends upon London. When he does you may
look for him in two places. He will either bo pouring over the pictures
in the National Gallery or delighting and amazing some Parliamentary
Committee with his views on agricultural credit. But to see him in his
native attic you must climb to the top floor of Plunkett House, in Mer-
rion Square, Dublin—the house presented to Sir Horace Plunkett in
recognition of his unique work for rural Ireland. There, in a congruous
disorder, jovial and inky and bespectacled, you will find *“ JE.” He
may be meditating a new religion or painting a picture on the wall or
writing a leader for the ‘‘ Irish Homestead.”” But whatever he is doing
he will infallibly be striving and failing to keep his pipe alight.

SYDNEY BROOKS.

There are a few noted Irishmen that for varying reasons it is a
rather subtle and interesting shock to meet 1n the streets of Dublin.
One is the hardworking mystic “ A. E.,”” who will stop and talk to ws
on Samothrace or Liffey dredging, on patangah or the latest facts
from potato fields. He conveys the impression that everything in Dub-
lin is at once profoundly important and merely incidental, related on
one side to eternal mystery and beauty, on the other to everlasting
irony,

Arvother is Padriac Colum. He seems even as a country boy who
has just seen the city for the first time, but who is shyly and patheti-
cally anxious to run away from it. A deer that has strayed into O’Con-
rell Street or Grafton Street could not be more bewildered or more
anvious to escape to the hills. He always lookd gently and pootically

incongruous in: Dublin, and alwaye 1n the act of taking a last look at it
and a hurrying farewell to it, . W. P. RYAN.
